text: Last class you walked the audience through each point with a clear overview.
Today you make the numbers on those slides tell a clear story.

Our growth was faster than the market.
This figure is much higher than last year.
Costs are more efficient than before.
Our churn is as low as the best in the segment.
Okay, Eduardo, show me your data slide. Tell me the story, not just the numbers.
Sure. Revenue rose sharply to a record figure of sixty-five million, which is much higher than last year.
Good. But you have ten numbers on that slide. Which one is the story?
The trend. Over the year we grew far faster than the market, and our cost per lead halved.
Exactly. Then highlight that one figure, and hide the rest. Let the trend do the talking.
Got it. One key figure per slide, one clear comparison, one simple message.
Let me walk you through this chart.
As you can see, the trend is clearly upward.
The key figure to remember is this one.
This is much higher than last year.
In short, the numbers tell a strong story.

Let me walk you through the numbers on this chart. As you can see, revenue rose sharply in the first quarter, from forty million to sixty-five million. That is the highest figure in our history. After that, growth was steady through the second quarter, with a small dip in April. The most important point is the trend: over the full year, our revenue grew by almost fifty percent, which is far stronger than the market average. The blue line shows our company, and the gray line shows our main competitor. Notice that the gap between the two lines is much wider than last year. In short, we are not only growing, we are growing faster than everyone else in the segment.

Okay, let me rehearse the data part of my talk one more time. I want to make the numbers tell a story, not just read them. So, instead of saying the revenue was sixty-five million, I will say revenue rose sharply to a record sixty-five million. I should compare, too. Our growth was twice as fast as the market, and our cost per lead was much lower than last year. I think I am using too many figures on one slide. The audience cannot follow ten numbers at once. I need to highlight one key figure per slide and let the trend do the talking. Slower, fewer numbers, one clear message each time. That is how you make data come alive.

First, you show the chart and tell the audience what they are looking at. Then you point to the overall trend, so people see the big picture. After that, you highlight the single most important figure on the slide. Next, you compare it with last year or with the market to give it meaning. Finally, you sum up the story the numbers tell in one clear sentence.

In short, the numbers tell a strong story.

This is much higher than last year.
